headline: Norwegian Engineers Blast Path to Europe's Longest Undersea Tunnel
meta_description: At 16.6 miles long, Norway's Rogfast tunnel will cut a 5-hour coastal drive to under an hour, featuring a luminous underground hall to keep drivers alert.
summary_short: Deep beneath the North Sea, Norwegian crews are carving out the world's longest undersea road tunnel—complete with an artfully lit great hall.
body:
Beneath the cold waters of the North Sea, Norwegian engineers are blasting through rock 1,000 feet below the surface, inching closer to completing the world's longest undersea road tunnel. The Rogfast—short for Rogaland Fixed Link—will stretch 16.6 miles, surpassing Norway's own previous record-holder, the 9-mile Ryfylke tunnel.

The transformation will be dramatic for coastal travelers. Today, the 40-minute crow-flies distance between Stavanger and Bergen requires a grueling 5-hour drive with two ferry rides. Once complete in 2033, the journey will take substantially under an hour through four submerged lanes running 300 meters below the fjords.

The work is punishing. Contractors work 12-hour shifts for 12 consecutive days before taking 16 days to recover topside. Each blast advances the tunnel by just 5 to 6 yards. Engineers continuously test the rock ahead for hardness and water intrusion, since conditions shift unpredictably from granite-hard to soil-soft, requiring different support structures accordingly.

But Norwegian ambition goes beyond mere utility. Deep in the tunnel lies a remarkable feature: a vast underground hall illuminated in vibrant, shimmering colors. Designed to prevent driver fatigue during the monotonous drive, the space will eventually host artist installations. From 85 million cubic meters of excavated rock, Norway will even build an artificial island—transforming tunnel waste into community asset.
